insights: High-cost Server and Web Server assets (≥$8000) receive only ~12-month warranties—37% below the overall portfolio median (19.19 months) and 50% below their own category median (~24 months)—representing a material misalignment in value protection for the most expensive infrastructure assets.

Low-cost Storage Devices and Computer Peripherals (≤$223.70) consistently receive 24–36 month warranties, well above both the overall and category medians, indicating an inverse cost-warranty relationship that contradicts standard asset risk-protection logic.

Warranty duration assignment for high-cost servers is effectively random across all observable dimensions: no systematic differences exist by vendor, model (e.g., Dell PowerEdge R740 appears with 12-, 24-, and 36-month warranties), department, purchase date (Apr 2023–Mar 2024 overlap), or batch—suggesting absence of a documented, enforced procurement or contract policy.

The 16 high-cost short-warranty assets ($134,202 exposure, 3.2% of portfolio) are exclusively Server (3) and Web Server (13) models, confirming concentration of warranty misalignment in mission-critical infrastructure rather than peripheral or general-purpose equipment.

Within identical vendor-model combinations, 23 out of 24 groups with ≥3 assets show inconsistent warranty durations, and temporal analysis reveals no clustering (correlation = -0.24), confirming that warranty terms are not governed by time-based procurement cycles, vendor negotiations, or rolling policy updates.

The Computer category exhibits the highest count of cost-warranty misalignments (46), driven by both high-cost units receiving short warranties and low-cost units receiving long ones—indicating broader systemic inconsistency beyond just server-class assets.
summary: The analysis reveals a clear and financially material misalignment between asset cost and warranty duration: high-cost servers—critical infrastructure representing $134K in exposure—receive only ~12-month warranties, far below both portfolio and category medians, while low-cost peripherals receive disproportionately long coverage. This inverse relationship contradicts sound risk-based procurement logic. Critically, no observable dimension—including vendor, model, department, purchase timing, or batch—explains the variation; warranty assignment for identical hardware is inconsistent and uncorrelated with time or structure, indicating absence of a coherent, enforceable policy. The randomness extends across the portfolio, with 23 of 24 vendor-model groups showing internal warranty variance. These findings signal a governance gap in how warranty terms are selected and applied during procurement—particularly for high-value infrastructure—and recommend immediate review of purchasing workflows, contract templates, and vendor negotiation practices to align warranty length with asset criticality and replacement cost.
